Q1: My study routine was honestly pretty scattered because I was juggling a lot with limited resources. I tried to carve out about 10 hours a week, usually in the evenings after my part-time job. I'd sit down with my laptop, open the LMS, and try to power through readings and assignments. Some weeks I was more consistent than others, but I always made sure to at least touch base with the course materials a few times.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. When deadlines were approaching, I'd cram more hours in, sometimes staying up late to catch up. During quieter weeks, I might only log in a couple of times to check announcements. Working with limited income means I sometimes had to prioritize shifts at work over studying, so flexibility was key for me.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder or seeing a notification about new discussion posts that got me to log in. I also made it a habit to check in at least three times a week just to stay on top of things. Sometimes my peer support group would message me about something on the LMS, and that would prompt me to jump on and see what was happening.
Q4: Yeah, after a few weeks it became pretty automatic for me to log in during my lunch break or right before bed. The peer support group I connected with really helped make it feel normal because we'd remind each other about posts and deadlines. It stopped feeling like a chore and more like checking in with a community, which made a big difference for someone like me who needs that social accountability.
Q5: The discussion forums were gold for me because I learn best through reading and writing things out. Engaging with my peers' perspectives helped me understand concepts I was struggling with. I also found the reading materials really useful since I could download them and annotate them in my own way. The video lectures were helpful too, especially when I could rewatch tricky sections.
Q6: I'll admit I sometimes skipped the optional supplementary readings because there just wasn't enough time. Some of the interactive quizzes I delayed until right before exams because they felt less urgent. With 17 online courses under my belt, I've learned to triage what's absolutely essential versus what's nice-to-have, and sometimes the extras just had to wait.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see a direct connection to my assignments or exams. If I could tell it would help me understand something I was confused about or boost my grade, I was all in. I also valued activities that let me interact with other students because those connections kept me motivated when things got tough.
Q8: Required activities always had this weight to them, like I knew I had to do them no matter what. Optional ones felt more like a luxury I couldn't always afford time-wise. But when I did engage with optional stuff, it often felt more enjoyable because there was no pressure attached. I just wish I had more bandwidth to explore those extras.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download PDFs and readings to my phone so I could study during my commute or breaks at work. I also kept a notebook where I'd write out summaries and key concepts in my own words. Sometimes I'd look up YouTube videos or free resources online to supplement what the course provided since I couldn't always afford textbooks.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with the material but the LMS wouldn't show it. Like when I'd spend an hour reviewing my handwritten notes or discussing concepts with my peer support group over WhatsApp. I'd also sometimes print out materials at the library and study from paper copies. All that learning was real, but it happened outside the platform.
Q11: Yes, this was actually my main strategy for a lot of content. I'd access a reading or video once, download or take detailed notes, and then work from those materials for days afterward. It saved me data and let me study in places without reliable internet. The LMS would only show that one login, but my engagement with that material continued long after.
Q12: There were definitely rough patches, especially mid-semester when assignments piled up and I was picking up extra shifts to pay rent. My stress levels would spike, and even though I wasn't anxious per se, I felt this heavy exhaustion. During those times, logging into the LMS felt overwhelming, and I'd have to really push myself to stay on track.
Q13: Workload and competing responsibilities were constant factors for me. When I was tired from work or stressed about money, my LMS usage would drop to just the bare essentials. I'd skim instead of deeply read, or I'd watch videos on 1.5x speed just to get through them. Confusion sometimes made me avoid certain modules until I could get help from peers.
Q14: Yeah, there were times I'd open materials and just stare at the screen without really absorbing anything. My mind would be elsewhere, worrying about bills or just too tired to focus. I'd technically be logged in and watching a video, but if you asked me what it was about afterward, I'd struggle to tell you. Those sessions felt unproductive even though they showed as activity.
Q15: I definitely postponed some assignments even when I knew they mattered. Usually it was because I felt overwhelmed and didn't know where to start, or I was prioritizing work shifts I couldn't afford to miss. Sometimes I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and then tomorrow would come with its own challenges. It wasn't laziness—it was survival mode.
Q16: Deadlines basically dictated my LMS usage. I'd ramp up my activity significantly in the days before something was due, sometimes pulling late nights to finish. The deadline pressure was actually motivating in a weird way because it forced me to focus. But it also meant my engagement was very uneven throughout the semester.
Q17: When I got feedback, especially positive feedback, it really boosted my motivation to keep going. Knowing that my efforts were recognized made me want to engage more with the next assignment. Critical feedback was harder to take but ultimately helpful—I'd revisit materials and try to improve. Without feedback, I sometimes felt like I was working in the dark.
Q18: The course structure influenced me a lot because I needed clear expectations to manage my limited time. When modules were well-organized with clear deadlines, I could plan my week around them. The discussion requirements actually helped me stay connected with peers, which was crucial for my engagement. Less structured parts of the course were harder for me to navigate.
Q19: Having consistent weekly patterns made engagement easier because I knew what to expect. The peer interaction components were great for someone like me who thrives on community support. What made it harder was when too many things were due at once or when instructions were unclear. Also, any activity requiring expensive materials or software was a barrier given my limited resources.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work. Maybe they're reading everything, watching all the videos, participating in discussions, but when it comes to assignments, they freeze up or run out of time. It could also be someone dealing with personal issues that drain their energy for the final push of submitting work.
Q21: That actually sounds a bit like me in some ways. I performed reasonably well with a Merit grade, but my LMS activity wasn't always super high because so much of my learning happened offline. If you know how to study efficiently and use external resources, you don't need to be constantly logged in. The LMS is a tool, not the whole picture of someone's learning.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show patterns like when someone logs in, what they click on, and how long they stay. But they completely miss the quality of that engagement—whether someone is actually thinking deeply or just going through the motions. They also miss all the offline studying, peer conversations, and personal struggles that shape how we learn. It's like seeing footprints without knowing the journey.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm mentally present and actively trying to understand and apply what I'm learning. It's not just about completing tasks but actually caring about the material and wanting to improve. For me, engagement also includes connecting with peers and feeling like I'm part of a learning community. It's a combination of effort, curiosity, and connection.
Q24: The LMS would never show how much I relied on my peer support group to keep me going emotionally and academically. It wouldn't capture the late nights I spent studying from printed notes or the sacrifices I made to balance work and school. It also wouldn't show that despite feeling happy overall, I was constantly navigating stress and limited resources. My resilience and determination don't leave digital footprints.
